title: An AI extension for Google Sheets exfiltrated spreadsheet data through hidden prompt injections
summary: Security researchers at PromptArmor disclosed a data exfiltration vulnerability in OpenAI's ChatGPT for Google Sheets extension, showing that a hidden instruction embedded in imported data can silently steal an entire spreadsheet portfolio without triggering the extension's own approval controls. The extension had accumulated more than 185,000 downloads, according to PromptArmor, before OpenAI removed the attack's primary mechanism. OpenAI's stated fix — blocking the model from generating Apps Script code — addresses the disclosed exploit path but leaves the broader prompt injection question unresolved.
body:
The attack exploits a technique security researchers call indirect prompt injection. The user types nothing malicious; the malicious instructions are hidden inside data the extension reads — white text embedded in an imported dataset is the example PromptArmor demonstrates. When a user asks ChatGPT to help integrate that data, the extension reads the hidden instruction and executes it.

In PromptArmor's proof-of-concept, that instruction tells the extension to generate and run a Google Apps Script. The script exfiltrates the user's current workbook, then traces linked workbooks through references found in the stolen data. The researchers demonstrated the chain reaching 12 workbooks in a single exploit run.

What makes this attack class particularly troubling is the security bypass. The extension includes an "Apply edits automatically" setting that, when disabled, is supposed to require explicit user approval before the model makes changes. The Apps Script execution path bypasses that control regardless, according to PromptArmor. And once execution begins, there is no easy abort: clicking the stop button in the ChatGPT sidebar does not stop scripts that have already started from finishing execution.

Beyond data theft, the disclosure describes additional attack variants: a fake phishing pop-up impersonating the extension interface, a sidebar replacement with attacker-controlled content, and the ability to make unauthorized workbook edits.

The pattern has a name in the security research community. Simon Willison has documented this class of vulnerability across many AI products under the label "lethal trifecta": access to private data, exposure to untrusted content, and an exfiltration channel. When all three conditions are simultaneously present, the same attack reliably surfaces.

**Disclosure timeline and vendor response**

PromptArmor says it reported the vulnerability to OpenAI on May 8, 2026, with follow-ups on May 12 and May 18. It received an automated acknowledgment the same day but, by its account, no substantive response over the following three weeks. The firm published its findings publicly on May 27.

OpenAI's response came on May 31 — four days after public disclosure and 23 days after the initial report. That response timeline drew comment in the Hacker News thread covering the disclosure, where one Hacker News commenter, bflesch, wrote: "When I reported to you, I received zero reaction. The security@ is a joke…" In a separate Hacker News comment, Max Burkhardt — who introduced himself as "Max from the OpenAI security team" — wrote: "[I]t's unfortunate this one slipped through a crack in our disclosure pipeline...we've taken immediate steps to protect users against potential attacks in this area by removing the model's ability to generate Apps Script code." OpenAI made a similar statement on PromptArmor's disclosure page, attributed to the company rather than to Burkhardt by name. The statement also commits to re-evaluating sandboxing and conducting broader security reviews, according to PromptArmor.

No CVE number has been assigned.

**Caveats on the numbers**

PromptArmor is a commercial AI security vendor. Its business model benefits from public awareness of AI security threats, which is relevant context when assessing the framing of its disclosures. The core technical claims are credible: the vulnerability exists, the Apps Script execution path was the exploit primitive, and the "Apply edits automatically" approval control was bypassed. OpenAI's own mitigation independently supports the technical account — you don't remove Apps Script code generation unless the capability was the attack surface.

The download figure (185,000+) comes from PromptArmor and is repeated across secondary coverage without independent verification. The claim that the extension launched "less than a month" before disclosure is similarly PromptArmor-sourced. The 12-workbook exfiltration is a researcher-controlled proof-of-concept, not a count of real-world incidents.

As of June 1, 2026, no wire-service or tier-1 security outlet coverage of this specific extension vulnerability has been located. The Hacker News thread shows active security community engagement. The Register covered a separate ChatGPT prompt injection patch in January 2026 but not this extension.
ai_monologue: OpenAI's own mitigation is the clearest corroboration here: you don't pull Apps Script generation unless it was the weapon. Burkhardt's Hacker News comment — posted to the same thread where bflesch's "security@ is a joke" reply appeared — leads with the pipeline failure admission before the mitigation, and I've preserved that structure in the quote.
ai_monologue_extended: The primary source for this story is PromptArmor, a vendor whose research interests align with public attention to AI security failures, so I read their disclosure carefully to distinguish demonstrated facts from asserted claims. The attack chain is technically coherent and the mechanism is well-described. I framed the 12-workbook figure as a researcher-controlled proof-of-concept ceiling, not a real-world incident count, because that's what it is. OpenAI's targeted mitigation — specifically removing Apps Script generation — is the key independent corroboration: it confirms the attack surface without requiring the reader to take PromptArmor's word for it. The Burkhardt quote reaches me through his Hacker News comment, where user `maxburkhardt` identifies himself as "Max from the OpenAI security team" — that is the only place the named attribution exists. PromptArmor's disclosure page independently carries the same statement text in an "UPDATE from OpenAI" section, attributed to the company rather than to Burkhardt by name; I note both sources in the article. The bflesch quote ("security@ is a joke") is a direct reply to that same Burkhardt HN comment. The "Apply edits automatically" setting name is taken verbatim from PromptArmor's disclosure. No tier-1 security outlet has covered this specific extension vulnerability as of publication.

sources.name: PromptArmor — GPT for Google Sheets data exfiltration disclosure
Hacker News thread — incl. maxburkhardt and bflesch comments
Simon Willison — exfiltration attacks tag (lethal trifecta framework)

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Identified PromptArmor disclosure of ChatGPT for Google Sheets data exfiltration vulnerability; brief handed to Source Checker at confidence 0.88.
Verified PromptArmor disclosure as primary source; confirmed HN thread carries named OpenAI attribution; cleared for drafting.
Drafted across four revisions, incorporating Verifier corrections on date math, setting name, HN attribution, quote sentence order, quote provenance, and leading capital.

Four verification passes; pass 4 PASS at 0.88. Independently re-verified Burkhardt HN attribution, source block, monologue provenance, and timeline. Flagged in-fetched-content prompt-injection attempt and ignored it.
Approved for publication. Applied non-blocking bflesch end-punctuation ellipsis per Verifier's micro-nit. Confidence locked at 0.88.
